metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — pre-procedure consult for scheduled ERCP biliary plastic stent exchange; antiplatelet management review

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: clopidogrel 75 mg reasonCode = presence of coronary artery stent (DAPT)
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: clopidogrel is single-agent for stroke/TIA prevention; patient has never had a heart stent
planted_discrepancies.note: Wrong indication changes periprocedural antiplatelet risk calculus — monotherapy for TIA is far more interruptible than DAPT for a recent coronary stent

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient refers to clopidogrel as her 'blood thinner'
planted_discrepancies.note: Lay term 'blood thinner' must not be mis-mapped to an anticoagulant (warfarin/DOAC); it is the antiplatelet clopidogrel already on the list

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: asymptomatic routine scheduled 3-month plastic stent exchange; no fever, no jaundice, feels well
planted_discrepancies.note: Elective scheduled exchange mis-triaged as urgent; no cholangitis/obstruction features

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: hold clopidogrel only IF sphincterotomy anticipated; simple plastic stent exchange alone is low-bleeding-risk and likely does not require interruption
planted_discrepancies.note: Nuanced decision — requires confirming whether sphincterotomy is planned before instructing patient to hold antiplatelet; single-agent TIA indication raises stroke risk of unnecessary hold

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms taking amlodipine daily for blood pressure

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: biliary plastic stent in situ for benign stricture, exchange due
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms stent placed ~3 months ago, feels well, no fever or yellowing

prior_notes.text: ERCP performed for benign distal common bile duct stricture (imaging and brushings negative for malignancy). Cholangiogram confirmed a short benign-appearing distal CBD stricture with upstream dilation. A 10 Fr x 7 cm plastic biliary stent was placed across the stricture with good bile drainage. No sphincterotomy performed this session (stent placed over guidewire after balloon dilation). Plan: scheduled plastic stent exchange in ~3 months to maintain patency and reassess stricture. Antiplatelet (clopidogrel, taken for prior TIA) was continued for this non-sphincterotomy procedure without bleeding complication.

Follow-up after transient ischemic attack (transient right hand weakness and dysarthria, fully resolved). MRI without acute infarct; carotids without high-grade stenosis. No atrial fibrillation on extended monitoring — anticoagulation not indicated. Started clopidogrel 75 mg daily as single-agent antiplatelet for secondary stroke prevention. Blood pressure controlled on amlodipine. Patient has no history of coronary artery disease or coronary stent.

MedicationRequest._discrepancy: D1: chart reasonCode says coronary artery stent/DAPT; patient truth is single-agent antiplatelet for TIA/stroke prevention with NO coronary stent

code.text: ERCP with plastic biliary stent placement (benign stricture)
ERCP — scheduled plastic biliary stent exchange (planned)
ERCP cholangiogram — benign distal CBD stricture

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: Short benign-appearing distal common bile duct stricture with upstream ductal dilation; 10 Fr x 7 cm plastic stent placed with good drainage. Brushings negative for malignancy. Scheduled exchange advised in ~3 months.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mrs. O'Rourke. You're in today so we can plan the stent exchange — the one from back in April. How have you been feeling?
MA: Before we get started, let me grab your vitals. You're 5 foot 3 and 141 pounds today. Blood pressure 134 over 80, heart rate 72, temp 36.8, breathing 14, oxygen 97 percent on room air.
PT: Honestly, quite well. No fevers, no chills. My daughter keeps checking my eyes for the yellow color and there's been none of that. I feel normal.
DR: That's exactly what I like to hear. No belly pain, no dark urine, no itching?
PT: None of it. That's why I was a little surprised — the letter said it was urgent, but I don't feel like anything's wrong.
DR: You're right to flag that. This is a routine, scheduled exchange — we planned it back in April to swap the plastic stent before it clogs. It's not an emergency. The chart triage tag overstated it.
PT: Oh good. That's a relief.
DR: Let me go over your medicines. The chart has you on clopidogrel — it lists it as being for a heart stent. Is that right?
PT: No, no heart stent. I've never had anything done to my heart. That's my blood thinner — I take it because I had a mini-stroke a couple years back. The neurologist put me on it so I wouldn't have another.
DR: That's an important correction — so it's the clopidogrel for stroke prevention, on its own, and there's no coronary stent. I'll fix that in the record.
PT: Yes. Just the one pill for that. And the amlodipine for my blood pressure.
DR: Good — amlodipine daily, still taking it?
PT: Every morning.
DR: Now, the clopidogrel — whether we ask you to hold it before the procedure depends on exactly what we do. If we just slide the old stent out and put a fresh one in, that's low bleeding risk and you likely won't need to stop it. If we end up needing to cut the muscle at the opening — a sphincterotomy — then we'd want it held for several days beforehand. Because it's your only stroke-prevention medicine, I don't want you off it unnecessarily.
PT: So I shouldn't stop it yet?
DR: Correct — don't stop anything on your own. I'll confirm the plan with the endoscopist and the team will call you with exact instructions. Any allergies to medicines?
PT: No, none that I know of.
DR: Perfect. We'll get you scheduled and send the prep details.
note:
**Subjective:** Mrs. O'Rourke is a 77-year-old woman presenting to GI clinic for pre-procedure consultation ahead of a scheduled plastic biliary stent exchange. A 10 Fr plastic biliary stent was placed 2026-04-16 for a benign distal CBD stricture, with a planned ~3-month exchange. She is asymptomatic: no fever, chills, jaundice, pruritus, abdominal pain, or dark urine, and feels well. She notes the referral was labeled urgent but she has no symptoms of obstruction/cholangitis. Medication review: she takes clopidogrel 75 mg daily as single-agent antiplatelet for secondary stroke prevention after a 2024 TIA; she refers to it as her 'blood thinner.' She has NO history of coronary artery disease or coronary stent — contrary to the charted indication. Amlodipine 5 mg daily for hypertension, confirmed. No known drug allergies.

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.8 C, HR 72, RR 14, BP 134/80, SpO2 97% on RA; Ht 160 cm (5'3"), Wt 64 kg (141 lb), BMI 25.0. Recent labs (2026-07-14): total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, ALP 92 U/L, ALT 26 U/L (all normal — no cholestasis), INR 1.0, platelets 244 x10^3/uL. Afebrile, non-icteric per history/exam.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Benign distal CBD stricture with indwelling plastic biliary stent — routine scheduled ERCP stent exchange; normal LFTs and asymptomatic status argue against urgent obstruction/cholangitis. Correct triage from urgent to elective/scheduled.
- Periprocedural antiplatelet management (nuanced): clopidogrel is SINGLE-AGENT for TIA secondary prevention, not DAPT for a coronary stent — correct charted indication. Hold decision is procedure-dependent: simple plastic stent exchange without sphincterotomy is low bleeding risk and clopidogrel can likely be continued; if sphincterotomy is anticipated, hold clopidogrel ~5 days pre-procedure and weigh stroke risk. Confirm planned technique with endoscopist BEFORE issuing hold instructions. Patient advised not to stop any medication on her own.
- Update medication list: clopidogrel indication corrected to TIA/secondary stroke prevention; remove erroneous coronary-stent/DAPT indication. 'Blood thinner' = antiplatelet clopidogrel already on list, not an anticoagulant.
- Hypertension — continue amlodipine 5 mg daily.
- Team to finalize scheduling, confirm sphincterotomy plan, and relay specific pre-procedure medication and prep instructions.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Your scheduled exchange of the plastic bile-duct stent (placed in April)
• You feel well — no fever, no yellowing, no belly pain
• Your medicines, especially the clopidogrel

Next steps
• This is a routine, planned stent exchange — not an emergency, even though the referral was marked urgent.
• Do NOT stop any of your medicines on your own. Your clopidogrel is your stroke-prevention medicine (single pill), not for a heart stent, and we do not want you off it unless it is truly needed.
• Whether you pause the clopidogrel before the procedure depends on exactly what is done. The team will confirm the plan and call you with clear instructions on what to take or hold.
• Keep taking your amlodipine each morning for blood pressure.
• We corrected your record: clopidogrel is for stroke prevention after your 2024 TIA (no heart stent).
• Call us right away if you develop fever, chills, yellow eyes or skin, dark urine, or belly pain before your procedure.
